MASTER CALENDAR COURT
Off Calendar Nov-09-2018 Vacated

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Dec-27-2017 Continued To Feb-14-18 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-13-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT ZYNGA, INC.'s MOTION TO STRIKE FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT, as to of the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Christian Larsen is granted. No opposition filed. The prevailing party shall submit a [proposed] written order that tracks the Court's ruling. A proposed order is submitted with the moving papers, but is not yet signed.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Jose Rios Merida; Reporter: Not Reported. (D302)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-13-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT ZYNGA, INC.'s DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT, as to the third through sixth causes of action in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Christian Larsen is OVERRULED as to the third cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress and SUSTAINED without leave to amend as the fourth cause of action for negligent infliction of emotional distress, the fifth cause of action negligence and the sixth cause of action for violation of the Unfair Competition Law. Liberally construing the complaint, it adequately alleges extreme and outrageous conduct by Zynga to support an IIED claim. That claim is not limited to normal personnel decisions and is thus not precluded by Janken v. GM Hughes Electronics (1996) 46 Cal.App.4th 55, 80. Among other things, the FAC alleges that Zynga intentionally ignored Mr. Larsen's complaint about serious fire hazards that caused Mr. Larsen to fear for his personal safety and his employment was terminated because he reported the fire hazards. Nor is the IIED claim barred by the exclusivity provision of the workers' compensation statutes because Mr. Larsen alleges actionable retaliation and wrongful termination in violation of public policy. (Fretland v. County of Humboldt (1999) 69 Cal. App. 4th 1478, 1492). The negligent infliction of emotional distress and negligence causes of action fail because the FAC alleges intentional misconduct, not negligent misconduct. (Semore v. Pool (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d 1087, 1105.) The negligence cause of action also fails because there is no nexus between the alleged breach (unsafe work environment) and the alleged harm (termination of employment). Mr. Larsen alleges that his fire hazard complaints, not the unsafe fire exit, were the reason his employment was terminated. The UCL cause of action fails because Mr. Larsen has not alleged, nor can he reasonably allege, any grounds for either restitution or injunctive relief. Written order and judgment are signed and filed in open court.

Judge: Harold Kahn: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Giselle Casey, CSR No. 8098, e-mail: casey.giselle@gmail.com (L&M302)
